semantic_journey: Arabic is the place name Samsat/Samosata. Greek is the identical toponym. Both name the same ancient city. This is proper place-name identity not a lexical root cognate. Arabic form derives from Greek or shared Aramaic substrate.
key_insight: Same place name (Samosata/Samsat) in Arabic and Greek - toponymic identity, not a root cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek forms are both loanwords from Latin stabulum (from stare = to stand). Neither is a native Semitic root meaning stable. Shared Latin loanword transmitted to both Arabic and Greek from Roman culture.
key_insight: Both Arabic and Greek forms are loanwords from Latin stabulum - shared Latin loanword, not Arabic-origin.

semantic_journey: Arabic shamshir and Greek form both borrow from Persian shamshir (curved sword). Arabic borrowed from Persian; Greek borrowed from Persian or via Arabic. Shared Persian loanword not a native Arabic root cognate.
key_insight: Both Arabic and Greek forms come from Persian shamshir - shared Persian loanword, not native Arabic.

semantic_journey: Arabic sesame (Sesamum indicum). Greek sesamon. The Semitic root s-s-m names this Near Eastern domesticated oil crop, attested in Akkadian as shamashshammu. Greek borrowed the word directly from Semitic via agricultural trade - the crop and its name traveled together from ancient Near East to the Mediterranean.
key_insight: Semitic sesame term (s-s-m) entered Greek via ancient crop trade - textbook material culture Semitic-to-Greek loanword.

semantic_journey: Arabic carrot term is explicitly acknowledged as borrowed FROM Greek. Direction of borrowing is Greek-to-Arabic. Presenting this as Arabic-to-Greek inverts the historical flow. The Arabic term is a Greek loanword.
key_insight: Arabic carrot term borrowed FROM Greek - direction of borrowing inverted in the original scoring.

semantic_journey: Arabic lute term is stated to be borrowed from Greek/Iranian. Arabic received the term from Greek. Direction of transmission runs toward Arabic. Not an Arabic-origin cognate for Greek.
key_insight: Arabic lute term borrowed from Greek/Iranian - transmission runs Greek-to-Arabic, not the reverse.

semantic_journey: Greek word derives from native Greek verb (to chew/gnaw), named because islanders chewed this resin. Arabic borrowed the term FROM Greek. The Greek word is native in origin; Arabic received it as a loanword.
key_insight: Greek mastic word from native Greek verb; Arabic borrowed FROM it - Greek-to-Arabic direction.

semantic_journey: Arabic form is a morphological variant of the same Greek loanword for carrot (same as GRC_0004). Arabic borrowed from Greek, not the reverse. Duplicate issue.
key_insight: Variant of GRC_0004 - Arabic carrot term borrowed from Greek with inverted borrowing direction.
